lines.translation: Winter break is almost here. For the past few days, Zhang Tianming has been thinking about taking a trip. His parents want him to visit their hometown, Nanjing. Zhang Tianming would also like to be Lisa's tour guide and take her to a few places in China. Besides Nanjing, where else should they go? To decide on the itinerary, Zhang Tianming took out a map of China and started to look at it.
Lisa, come take a look at the map of China. Let's see where would be a good place to travel.
Look at a map? That's a good idea. I've been wanting to learn a little about Chinese geography, too. I know too little about Chinese geography.
Let's first go to Nanjing, my parents' hometown... It's here, in southeastern China. Then where do you want to go?
This is Harbin, right? Let's go to Harbin.
You want to go from the southeast to the northernmost part all of a sudden?
Harbin's ice lanterns were featured on TV yesterday. They're gorgeous.
The ice lanterns are beautiful, it's true, but Harbin is incredibly cold right now. Let's go in the summer.
Alright. If we're not going north, then let's take a train to the west. I hear Xinjiang is a very special place.
Xinjiang is in the northwest. It starts getting cold in October, so it's not a good time to go now. Besides, taking the train takes too much time. Let's go next time!
This is the Yangtze River, and this is the Yellow River. Let's take a boat to see the scenery of the Yangtze River or the Yellow River.
A boat trip would take a very long time. Most of China's rivers flow from west to east. If we travel from east to west, it'll take even longer.
Why do most of China's rivers flow from west to east?
The western part of China is plateaus; there, you'll find the world's tallest mountain. By contrast, in the east are plains and the ocean.

It seems that the big cities are all close to the coast or not far from the sea. Look, in the north, there are Beijing and Tianjin; in the southeast, here are Shanghai and Nanjing; and here in the south are Guangzhou and Shenzhen.

You put it correctly. China's population is primarily in the east and south. As for the southwest, there are numerous big mountains and plateaus. The northwest, on the other hand, has many deserts with unfavorable natural conditions. That's why the population there is relatively sparse.
I think China's topography is a bit like America's.

You have a point. Also, China and the U.S. are similar in area, but China's population is more than four times that of the U.S. I've heard that during holidays or vacations, tourist spots in China are packed with seas of people and extremely crowded.
Really?
Fortunately, when we get to China, the students won't be on vacation yet, and it won't be the New Year or other holidays.
China is so big, and our winter break is so short. Where would be the best place for us to visit?
I think there's no better place to go than Yunnan.
Where's Yunnan? ... I've found it. Here it is. It's a province in the southwest. Isn't it cold there in the winter?
I hear that some places in Yunnan are like spring all year round, neither too cold nor too hot.
I remember hearing on TV that Yunnan has beautiful scenery, many ethnic minorities, and is a great place for tourism.
Alright, Yunnan. It's decided. We'll go to Yunnan.

language_notes.label: a
language_notes.explanation_en: 旅行 and 旅游 are roughly synonymous. The term 旅行 does not by itself imply a purpose for travel, and travel agencies are always 旅行社. However, 游 means to wander, so 旅游 specifically implies traveling for pleasure. To travel for business is 出差 (chū chāi). Tourists are 游客. In Taiwan, the term 观光客 (guānguāngkè) is also used; 观光 means “sightseeing.”

language_notes.label: b
language_notes.explanation_en: 河流 is a collective noun. It can refer to rivers in general or all the rivers within a geographic area. 河 can refer to a specific river or several rivers.

language_notes.label: c
language_notes.explanation_en: 部 is used with other characters to form multisyllabic words such as 东部, 南部, 西南部. It is not used on its own. The term 中国的东部 (the eastern part of China) refers to the eastern region within China. By contrast, 中国的东边 can refer either to “the East of China” (i.e., within China) or “east of China” (i.e., outside of China).

language_notes.label: d
language_notes.explanation_en: 少数民族 means “ethnic minority” (lit. minority nationality).

grammar_sections.title: Indicating the beginning of a state using 起来
grammar_sections.explanation_en: As we learned in IC2, 起来 can indicate that an action is starting to take place. It can also be used after adjectives to indicate the start and continuation of a state. When a verb is followed by an object, the object must be placed between 起 and 来. 起来 is often used with adjectives such as 亮 (liàng, bright), 大 (big), 高 (high), 多 (many), 快 (fast), and 热闹 (rènao, lively). Like 起来, the complement 下来 can also follow a verb or adjective to indicate the start of an action or condition, but it usually suggests a diminution or decrease in size, volume, quantity, speed, intensity, etc. For instance, 下来 is often used with 暗 (àn, dark), 小 (small), 低 (low), 少 (few), 慢 (slow), 安静 (quiet), and 停 (stop). Seasonal weather changes can also be expressed with 起来.

examples.hanzi: 张天明拿出一张中国地图, 看了起来。
examples.pinyin: Zhāng Tiānmíng ná chū yì zhāng Zhōngguó dìtú, kànle qǐlái.
examples.translation: Zhang Tianming took out a map of China and started studying it.

examples.hanzi: 听她说完那些话, 大家都笑了起来。
examples.pinyin: Tīng tā shuō wán nàxiē huà, dàjiā dōu xiàole qǐlái.
examples.translation: After listening to what she said, everybody began to laugh.

examples.hanzi: 你怎么哭起来了?
examples.pinyin: Nǐ zěnme kū qǐlái le?
examples.translation: How come you started crying?

examples.hanzi: 吃完晚饭, 没有什么事儿, 他就看起杂志来。
examples.pinyin: Chī wán wǎnfàn, méiyǒu shénme shìr, tā jiù kàn qǐ zázhì lái.
examples.translation: After dinner he had a free moment, so he began to flip through some magazines.

examples.hanzi: 今天是周末, 购物中心里的人多起来了。
examples.pinyin: Jīntiān shì zhōumò, gòuwù zhōngxīn lǐ de rén duō qǐlái le.
examples.translation: It's the weekend today. The malls are starting to fill up with people.

examples.hanzi: 他走进房间打开灯, 房间里一下子亮起来了。
examples.pinyin: Tā zǒu jìn fángjiān dǎkāi dēng, fángjiān lǐ yíxiàzi liàng qǐlái le.
examples.translation: He walked into the room and turned on the light. The room brightened up instantly.

examples.hanzi: 夏天热起来了。
examples.pinyin: Xiàtiān rè qǐlái le.
examples.translation: Summer is starting to get hot.

examples.hanzi: 冬天冷起来了。
examples.pinyin: Dōngtiān lěng qǐlái le.
examples.translation: Winter is starting to get cold.

examples.hanzi: 刚才他开车开得很快, 进城以后, 他开得慢下来了。
examples.pinyin: Gāngcái tā kāichē kāi de hěn kuài, jìn chéng yǐhòu, tā kāi de màn xiàlái le.
examples.translation: Just now, he was driving very fast. After he entered the city, he began to slow down.

examples.hanzi: 屋子里的灯慢慢暗下来了。
examples.pinyin: Wūzi lǐ de dēng mànmàn àn xiàlái le.
examples.translation: The light in the room slowly began to dim.

examples.hanzi: 上课了, 教室里安静了下来。
examples.pinyin: Shàngkè le, jiàoshì lǐ ānjìng xiàlái le.
examples.translation: Class started, and the room began quieting down.

usage_notes: 起来 can follow a verb to indicate that an action is starting to take place.
When a verb that takes an object is used with 起来, the object must be placed between 起 and 来.
起来 can also be used after an adjective to indicate the start and continuation of a state.
起来 is often used with adjectives that suggest an increase or enhancement, such as 亮 (bright), 大 (big), 高 (high), 多 (many), 快 (fast), and 热闹 (lively).
In contrast, 下来 indicates the start of a condition that suggests a diminution or decrease. It is often used with adjectives like 慢 (slow), 暗 (dark), 小 (small), and 安静 (quiet).
Seasonal weather changes can be expressed with 起来, such as 夏天热起来了 (Summer is getting hot) and 冬天冷起来了 (Winter is getting cold).

grammar_sections.title: The conjunction 而
grammar_sections.explanation_en: 而 is used to connect two clauses that present different or contrasting characteristics or situations. It has a literary flavor.

patterns.meaning_en: Connects two clauses with different or contrasting characteristics/situations.

examples.hanzi: 中国西部是高原,那儿有世界上最高的山。而东边是平原和大海。
examples.pinyin: Zhōngguó xībù shì gāoyuán, nàr yǒu shìjiè shàng zuìgāo de shān. Ér dōngbiān shì píngyuán hé dàhǎi.
examples.translation: The western part of China is plateaus; there, you'll find the world's tallest mountain. By contrast, in the east are plains and the ocean.

examples.hanzi: 小林毕业以后计划回中国工作,而不准备留在美国。
examples.pinyin: Xiǎo Lín bìyè yǐhòu jìhuà huí Zhōngguó gōngzuò, ér bù zhǔnbèi liú zài Měiguó.
examples.translation: Little Lin plans to go back to China to work rather than stay in the United States.

examples.hanzi: 我想去学校图书馆打工,而我的同屋想去校外打工。
examples.pinyin: Wǒ xiǎng qù xuéxiào túshūguǎn dǎgōng, ér wǒ de tóngwū xiǎng qù xiàowài dǎgōng.
examples.translation: I want to work at the school library, but my roommate wants to work off campus.

examples.hanzi: 我哥哥已经拿到硕士学位了,而我才高中毕业。
examples.pinyin: Wǒ gēge yǐjīng nádào shuòshì xuéwèi le, ér wǒ cái gāozhōng bìyè.
examples.translation: My older brother has already gotten his master's degree. I, on the other hand, have just graduated from high school.

grammar_sections.title: 最 adjective 不过了
grammar_sections.explanation_en: “X最adjective 不过了” has the same meaning as “没有比X更adjective 的” (nothing can surpass X). It is a rather strong expression.

patterns.meaning_en: nothing is more... than...; ...is the best

examples.hanzi: 她过生日,买花送她最好不过了。
examples.pinyin: Tā guò shēngrì, mǎi huā sòng tā zuì hǎo búguò le.
examples.translation: Nothing would be a better gift for her birthday than flowers.

examples.hanzi: 这本书对世界经济的介绍最清楚不过了。
examples.pinyin: Zhè běn shū duì shìjiè jīngjì de jièshào zuì qīngchu búguò le.
examples.translation: This book's introduction to the global economy surpasses all others in clarity.

examples.hanzi: 这个实习工作对化学系的学生来说,最合适不过了。
examples.pinyin: Zhège shíxí gōngzuò duì huàxué xì de xuéshēng lái shuō, zuì héshì búguò le.
examples.translation: For a chemistry major, there's no internship more appropriate than this one.

grammar_sections.title: The dynamic particle 过 revisited
grammar_sections.explanation_en: As we learned in IC2, to express that someone has experience doing something, the dynamic particle 过 is used. It differs from 了 in two ways. First, 了 is descriptive in nature and is used to relate the occurrence of an action. 过, on the other hand, is explanatory in nature. It is used to explain the reasoning for a following statement. In other words, the emphasis is on the impact a past action has had on the present situation. The clauses containing 过 all serve as explanatory background to the clauses that follow. 过 indicates the impact or influence of a past action on the present. Second, 了 generally requires a specific time phrase, unless the sentence forms part of an extended narrative in which the temporal background has already been stated or is implicitly understood; 过 does not. When we use 过, the time implied is often rather vague: 'before' or 'in the past.' Only when we want to be more precise—usually, to refute or prove a point—do we use a time phrase.

examples.hanzi: 我去年去了一次中国，在北京实习了三个月，别的地方都没去，就回来了。
examples.translation: Last year, I went to China to intern in Beijing for three months. I really didn't go anywhere else, and then just came back.

examples.hanzi: 第二天早上，我很早就起来了，起床后就去运动场跑步。
examples.translation: The next morning I got up really early. After I got up, I went jogging at the athletic field.

examples.hanzi: 客人们进来以后，找到了自己的位子，坐了下来。
examples.translation: The guests walked in, found their seats, and sat down.

examples.hanzi: (以前)我们在一起学过英文，我知道他英文很好。
examples.translation: We used to study English together. I know his English is very good. (Because we studied English together, I know his English is very good.)

examples.hanzi:
你去过中国，请告诉我们去哪儿旅游最好。
云南的旅游景点多，少数民族的文化也很有意思，是旅游的好地方。
examples.translation:
You've been to China. Give us some tips on the best places to go. (Because you've been to China, you can give us some tips on where some of the best places to go are.)
Yunnan has plenty of tourist sights, and the cultures of the minority ethnic groups are also intriguing. It's a good place to visit.

examples.hanzi: 他学过好几年中文，都能看中文报纸、杂志了。
examples.translation: He's been studying Chinese for several years. He can even read Chinese newspapers and magazines. (Because he's been studying Chinese for several years, he can understand Chinese newspapers and magazines.)

examples.hanzi:
我听说你没去过别的国家，是真的吗？
谁说的？我去年去过日本。
examples.translation:
I heard that you've never been abroad. Is that true?
Who said that? I went to Japan last year.

usage_notes: When 过 is used in the experiential sense, it usually cannot be followed by 了.

grammar_sections.title: 为了 and 因为
grammar_sections.explanation_en: 为了 denotes purpose, whereas 因为 denotes cause.

examples.hanzi: 为了把中文学得更好,我明年要去中国留学。
examples.translation: In order to improve my Chinese, I'm going to study abroad in China next year.

examples.hanzi: 因为在中国学中文条件更好,所以我明年去中国留学。
examples.translation: Because the conditions in China are better for learning Chinese, I'm going to China to study next year.

examples.hanzi: 为了解决学费的问题,他一边上学一边打工。
examples.translation: In order to solve the problem of school tuition, he works part time while going to school.

examples.hanzi: 因为得交学费,所以他一边上学一边打工。
examples.translation: Because he has to pay for school tuition, he works part time while going to school.

examples.hanzi: 因为这个学校很有名,所以我申请。
examples.translation: Because this school is very famous, I'm applying to it.

examples.hanzi: 我申请这个学校,是为了能跟我的女朋友在一起。
examples.translation: I'm applying to this school so that I can be with my girlfriend.

grammar_sections.title: Comparing 理解 and 了解
grammar_sections.explanation_en: 理解 means “to understand.” 了解, on the other hand, means to have a clear knowledge of something.

examples.hanzi: 她现在学习压力大,不能帮助我,我理解。
examples.pinyin: Tā xiànzài xuéxí yālì dà, bù néng bāngzhù wǒ, wǒ lǐjiě.
examples.translation: She's under a lot of pressure with her studies and can't help me. I understand.

examples.hanzi: 老师刚才说的意思,你没有完全理解。
examples.pinyin: Lǎoshī gāngcái shuō de yìsi, nǐ méiyǒu wánquán lǐjiě.
examples.translation: You didn't completely understand what the teacher just said.

examples.hanzi: 做这件事很容易,我不理解你为什么怕这怕那。
examples.pinyin: Zuò zhè jiàn shì hěn róngyì, wǒ bù lǐjiě nǐ wèishéme pà zhè pà nà.
examples.translation: It's very easy to do this. I don't understand why you're being so timid.

examples.hanzi: 我在那个国家住过几年,对那里比较了解。
examples.pinyin: Wǒ zài nàge guójiā zhùguo jǐ nián, duì nàli bǐjiào liǎojiě.
examples.translation: I lived in that country for a few years, and know things there pretty well.

examples.hanzi: 你了解她的过去吗？
examples.pinyin: Nǐ liǎojiě tā de guòqù ma?
examples.translation: Do you know her past?

examples.hanzi: 我们老师对每个学生在学习上的问题都很了解。
examples.pinyin: Wǒmen lǎoshī duì měi ge xuéshēng zài xuéxí shàng de wèntí dōu hěn liǎojiě.
examples.translation: Our teacher has a clear knowledge of each student's problems with regard to studying.

grammar_sections.title: 一下子 (all of a sudden, in an instant)
grammar_sections.explanation_en: 一下子 is equivalent to 一下. It means “not much time has gone by,” and it's often used to depict how fast or how soon actions or things happen.

examples.hanzi: 你一下子就要从南边跑到最北边去？
examples.translation: All of a sudden, you want to go from the south to the northernmost part?

examples.hanzi: 你说的那个商店我一下子就找到了，不难找。
examples.translation: I found the store you mentioned right away. It wasn't difficult to find.

examples.hanzi: 妈妈给他的零用钱他一下子就花完了。
examples.translation: He went through the allowance that his mother had given him in no time.

grammar_sections.title: 大多 (mostly)
grammar_sections.explanation_en: 大多 is an adverb meaning “for the most part” or “mostly.”

examples.hanzi: 我们班的同学大多住在学校宿舍。
examples.pinyin: Wǒmen bān de tóngxué dàduō zhù zài xuéxiào sùshè.
examples.translation: Our classmates mostly live in the school dorms.

examples.hanzi: 美国的大山和河流的名字，我弟弟大多说不对。
examples.pinyin: Měiguó de dàshān hé héliú de míngzì, wǒ dìdi dàduō shuō bu duì.
examples.translation: My younger brother couldn't get the names of most of the big mountains and rivers of the U.S. right.

examples.hanzi: 我们学校教授的研究，我大多都了解，你不用上网看了。
examples.pinyin: Wǒmen xuéxiào jiàoshòu de yánjiū, wǒ dàduō dōu liǎojiě, nǐ búyòng shàngwǎng kàn le.
examples.translation: I'm familiar with most of our school's professors' research. You don't need to go online to find out.

usage_notes: It cannot be used before nouns.

grammar_sections.title: Indicating a pause in speech using 呢
grammar_sections.explanation_en: This particle occurs after a subject or topic and is followed by a pause in speech. It usually appears in enumerative sentences suggesting contrast.

patterns.meaning_en: Used to mark a pause after a topic, often in an enumerative or contrastive context.

examples.hanzi: 你说的没错,中国的人口主要在东部和南部。西南呢,高山、高原多,西北呢,沙漠多,自然条件不太好,所以人口比较少。
examples.pinyin: Nǐ shuō de méi cuò, Zhōngguó de rénkǒu zhǔyào zài dōngbù hé nánbù. Xīnán ne, gāoshān, gāoyuán duō, xīběi ne, shāmò duō, zìrán tiáojiàn bú tài hǎo, suǒyǐ rénkǒu bǐjiào shǎo.
examples.translation: You put it correctly. China's population is primarily in the east and south. As for the southwest, there are numerous big mountains and plateaus. The northwest, on the other hand, has many deserts with unfavorable natural conditions. That's why the population there is relatively sparse.

examples.hanzi:
Q: 你们寒假准备做什么?
A: 我呢,要回家,小张呢,要留在学校打工,小李要做什么,我不知道。
examples.pinyin:
Q: Nǐmen hánjià zhǔnbèi zuò shénme?
A: Wǒ ne, yào huí jiā, Xiǎo Zhāng ne, yào liú zài xuéxiào dǎgōng, Xiǎo Lǐ yào zuò shénme, wǒ bù zhīdào.
examples.translation:
Q: What do you plan to do over winter break?
A: I'm going home. Little Zhang, on the other hand, has to stay at school and work. I don't know what Little Li will do.

examples.hanzi: 选什么专业好,我还没想清楚。学医呢,没有意思,学文学呢,怕以后不好找工作。
examples.pinyin: Xuǎn shénme zhuānyè hǎo, wǒ hái méi xiǎng qīngchu. Xué yī ne, méiyǒu yìsi, xué wénxué ne, pà yǐhòu bù hǎo zhǎo gōngzuò.
examples.translation: What's a good major to choose? I haven't figured it out. Studying medicine, on the one hand, is boring. On the other hand, I'm worried that if I study literature, I won't be able to find a job.
